author: @riccardociannella2689
text:
air is very compressible but water is not, so the air obsorbes a lot of the energy and the springs back on the return. been a long time since I made any wave power generation but I liked playing with the idea of a big float (boat) with a fixed cable. as the boat goes down on the wave the tether retracted. as the boat lifts on the wave the generator is pulled and turned, simple but some big forces. every anchor on a big ship could harness this. I also tried out piezoelectric pads under the ocean floor near the shore line geared to small and big loads, small the waves on and of the shore line and big pezo for the tides, ( tides which is the elephant of the oceans powers i feel) all along harbor  / marina walls I see the waves pounding the concrete. its endless. a simple wall plate with a piston along the water break the endless miles of sea defenses . vast  amount of energy right next to where most poeple live on the coast. or again the pezo pad along the deferences. mile after mile of wasted concrete. 
 I was also devising changing the state of a gas from liquid to gas. so if you had a deep water tether and generator. 
the liquid changes to gas pulls the tether and so  generator to the surface, then change to liquid it retracts to the sea floor, the cycle goes round. with some minor buoyancy changes through the stages.
so many ways. I can think of at least 10 more. some a lot better than this lot , but just try and convince the powers that be and you will be ridiculed and sent on you way. 
If the idea can be realized by the person you show it to then it will 99% of the time be stolen.
whats the point in building yet another energy harvesting machine? really.
I tried everyone I could think of, so many companies. sharks sharks sharks.
I tried Caroline lucus my local GREEN MP. I asked to have the right to put a device in the water at Brighton beach so I could demonstrate how I could harvest energy from the ocean using super cheap off the shelf items available in any DIY shop. the device is not visible from the air, on land or in the water. cant be seen, has absolutely no impact on any living thing in, out or under the ocean floor. Zero impact, lots of energy, no visual impact and super cheap.
her answer was " If you vote for me in the next election we can look at helping start up like this.
and that was the best reaction I had from the many companies and government bodies I tried.
Its all out there for the taking but the impossible barrier I can not pass is the powers that control who is allowed to try and demonstrate this. maybe if I was a company paying vast amounts of money into party supporting stuff I would get access.
but at the end the day the years I put into finding simple solutions to our power problems was a complete waste of time.
there are thousands of people who spend years to find the answers only to be robbed or beaten down, waste of my time.
fun though to invent but work pays the bills not free energy. not in out system of greed

author: @MrFmiller
text: Wave harvesting is interesting but I am inclined to think tidal generation would be more practical. Consider that waves are generated at the surface and represent a minor fraction of the energy produced by the volume of the oceans. The tides contain far more potential energy. Think of a float with a cable anchored at one end going over the shaft of a generator attached to a weight to take up the slack, rising and falling with each cycle. The apparatus would be relatively impervious to the effects of weather and have very small environmental impact since it would just be a float and a weight rising and falling with the tides. Now scale that up to a massive floating structure with very large generators. Anchor the corners with columns embedded in the sea floor. My thought is there would be a minimum amount of components making it less prone to failure.

author: @mikewillemse3761
text:
Hi Robert 
for wave energy
build a chamber or use bellows
connect a valve, as per the air battery that will give you air pressure and a vacuum
Two turbines, one for the pressure side and the other for vacuum
connect both output shafts to a planetary gear system
connect to a fly wheel for constant rotation
and you should be able to generate power.

author: @janmalan471
text:
I used to be a diamond diver of the west coast of South Africa and Namibia where I worked close to land just behind the break water. So I have first hand experience of the power there is in what we called the "Surge" when a wave pass over head. If I were to build a wave power harnessing plant I will rather do it with a bank of underwater turbines that is driving centrifugal pumps that will pump water to one central turbine connected to a generator.

Your bellows could also be substituted with floats that is at the end of a rocker arm with a racking gear on, that in turn could drive two pinions with one way bearings driving a shaft that drives your generator.  

By the way, I find the content that you put up extremely interesting and informative, thank you. I would love to build a Zinc Bromide battery but do not find the raw materials needed to build the plates around here.

author: @energycrafts8206
text: Hi Robert, I have a possible improvement to your bellows making.  The front end engine drive couplings of a car  use a bellows like item to seal and protect the spline shaft movements from dirt and loss of lubrication.  You may have these in your scrapping out car or easy to get at any junkyard.  The advantages are:  designed for long duration and wear, can handle extreeme vibration, can handle much higher pressures than you have now and can have long extension strokes.  Might be the way to go for a more robust version to harness sea level to air pressure into bellow "pistons."

author: @stevenw4549
text:
It's interesting that I commented yesterday or the day before the idea that perhaps you should look into wave power using the ocean. And now you've incorporated that into your bellows device. Coincidence perhaps. At any rate it would be gratifying to think that you do get ideas from other people's suggestions. That's what the internet is all about I think, sharing thoughts and ideas. I was about to suggest using a piston instead of the bellows but someone beat me to it in the comments here lol oh well.
Thinking a piston might have less wear and tare and is more durable. Also I might add that they are currently using wave power by converting the wave energy directly onto mechanical motion using buoyancy and generating electricity that way instead of using changes in air pressure. Example: Paddles moving up and down with the waves. Must be a bunch of different ways to do that. Perhaps you might be interested in my idea for using the extreme pressures at ocean depths to launch rockets to save fuel?

author: @TheScoundrelM
text: I’m a complete layman so this is probably a question with an obvious answer, but I’m going to ask anyway. Would it be reasonable to use the kind of one way flow system used in a simple foot pump and either have a dual turbine system (one for each air direction) or, redirect the air to flow through one turbine in a single direction?

replies.author: @derekp2674
replies.text: I think the type of "conventional" oscillating water column device that Robert shows in the video would be regarded by some as an "advanced" design by virtue of its use of a single bidirectional air turbine instead of mechanically more complicated arrangements. For any viable commercial wave power generators, the need to provide moving mechanical systems can be a severe reliability and maintenance headache, hence designs that eliminate the need for mechanical rectification of any oscillating air flow can be regarded as advanced, at least if the bidirectional turbine design is good enough.

picture in your minds eye "doing both at the same time" is not the same as doing both at the same point/location  in space.. put another way, two operations at the same time ,in this case, one operation on the inlet (high pressure),the other operation on the outlet (lower pressure/vacuum) ( perhaps push/pull makes it simpler to see) gets you closer to optimal power conversion to use doing useful work with less waste locally.
